title: Checkpointing and at-least-once replay
summary: Explains how checkpointing supports recovery and why at-least-once delivery can repeat idempotent writes.

question_patterns: How does checkpointing prevent data loss?

nodes.label: Checkpoint progress
nodes.description: Progress in marking events as successfully processed so the pipeline can restart without losing changes.

nodes.label: Records awaiting acknowledgement
nodes.description: Records emitted by the source or processor that are waiting for checkpoint completion before they are acknowledged.

nodes.label: Duplicate write attempts
nodes.description: Possible repeated writes after restart or reprocessing under the at-least-once delivery guarantee.

nodes.label: Target data freshness
nodes.description: How up to date the Redis target database is compared with the source database.

edges.condition: Records have been processed and checkpoint completion advances.
edges.summary: Successful checkpoint progress reduces the number of records waiting for acknowledgement.

edges.condition: The processor fails before records are acknowledged.
edges.summary: Records awaiting acknowledgement can be replayed after failure, which preserves changes but may repeat target writes.
Under at-least-once delivery, duplicate writes may occur but should not change the final target state when writes are idempotent.

feedback_loops.label: At-least-once replay after failure
feedback_loops.summary: Checkpointing prevents data loss by allowing replay after processor failure. Replay can repeat writes, but idempotent writes preserve the target state.

answering_guidance: Use at-least-once delivery language: a change should not be lost, but it can be processed more than once.
Explain duplicate writes together with idempotent writes, not as corruption by default.
Use pending acknowledgement and checkpoint metrics when troubleshooting replay windows.
